title: Cat Weight Loss Despite Increased Appetite: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat eating more but losing weight? Discover common causes like hyperthyroidism and diabetes, and learn when to seek veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: A cat losing weight while eating well often indicates a metabolic condition like hyperthyroidism or diabetes; while not usually an immediate emergency, you should schedule a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours.

Why is my cat losing weight even though they are eating more than usual?

It can be deeply concerning to watch your cat eat ravenously while their body seems to be wasting away. This clinical combination—increased appetite (polyphagia) alongside weight loss—suggests that your cat's body is either unable to process the nutrients they consume or is burning through calories at an abnormally high rate due to a metabolic imbalance.

Is it an emergency if my cat is losing weight despite having a healthy appetite?

The urgency level for a cat losing weight despite a high appetite is Medium. While this is typically not a sudden life-threatening crisis that requires an ER visit tonight, it is a sign of a significant underlying medical condition that should be evaluated by a veterinarian within the next 24 to 48 hours. Seek immediate care if your cat also exhibits:

  • Extreme lethargy
  • Sudden blindness
  • Persistent vomiting

What are the common causes for weight loss and increased appetite in cats?

  • Hyperthyroidism: Most frequent in middle-aged and senior cats, where an overactive thyroid gland sends the metabolism into overdrive.
  • Diabetes Mellitus: A condition where the body cannot utilize blood sugar for energy.
  • Intestinal Malabsorption: Issues such as Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) or certain cancers that prevent nutrient uptake.
  • Intestinal Parasites: Heavy worm loads, which are a common consideration particularly in younger cats.

How does a photo of my cat help a veterinarian during the triage process?

Sharing a photo of your cat with a veterinary professional can be a vital part of the triage process. Providing a clear image helps the team assess the following:

  • Body Condition Score (BCS): A bird's-eye view taken from above while your cat is standing helps determine their overall condition.
  • Muscle Wasting: Visuals allow professionals to see the severity of muscle loss over the hips and spine.
  • Objective Physical State: Photos provide a more accurate measure of physical health than a weight number alone.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Common clinical signs associated with weight loss despite increased appetite (polyphagia) in cats include hyperthyroidism and malabsorption. Hyperthyroidism often presents with additional signs such as hyperexcitability, polydipsia, polyuria, palpable thyroid enlargement, vomiting, diarrhea, and cardiovascular abnormalities. In cases of malabsorption, chronic diarrhea may be present, and systemic signs may be minimal unless severe inflammation or neoplasia exists. Animals with malabsorption may exhibit coprophagia and pica, and palpable thickened bowel loops or enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes may be present, particularly in cats.

Chapter: Endocrinology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 559)

faqs.question: Can hyperthyroidism in cats be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes, hyperthyroidism is highly manageable through daily medication, a prescription diet, or a curative treatment called radioactive iodine therapy.

faqs.question: Is diabetes the only reason a cat would be hungry and thin?
faqs.answer: No, while diabetes is a common cause, other conditions like hyperthyroidism and gastrointestinal diseases present with almost identical symptoms.

faqs.question: What diagnostic tests will my vet likely recommend?
faqs.answer: Expect your vet to recommend a full blood panel (including thyroid levels), a urinalysis to check for glucose, and possibly a fecal exam to rule out parasites.
